title: Listening - Part 4: Retiring Early
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people discussing their decision to retire in their early fifties

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d spent thirty years in a high-pressure corporate law firm, and [21]I decided to retire early because I was starting to feel a profound sense of burnout and mental exhaustion[/21]. I just couldn't face another merger. [26]The most unexpected benefit has been the massive improvement in my physical health[/26]. Since I stopped working, my blood pressure has dropped significantly and I no longer suffer from chronic headaches. It’s been a physical rebirth that I didn't think was possible at my age. || Speaker 2: I’m a former architect, and [22]I retired at fifty-two because I wanted to devote all my time to my passion for landscape painting before my eyesight started to fail[/22]. I wanted to be a full-time artist. However, [27]the biggest challenge was the sudden loss of social status that came with leaving my profession[/27]. I found it very difficult to answer the question 'So, what do you do?' at parties. It took me a long time to feel comfortable identifying as an artist rather than an architect. || Speaker 3: We’d been living a very frugal life for years, so [23]my husband and I retired early because we wanted to travel the world while we were still physically active enough to enjoy it[/23]. We wanted an adventure. [28]What I found most difficult was the initial anxiety about our long-term financial security[/28]. Even though we’d planned meticulously, seeing the savings account go down instead of up was terrifying for the first few months. I had to learn to trust our math and stop checking the stock market every hour. || Speaker 4: I’d been a senior manager in a hospital, and [24]I retired early because I wanted to be more present for my elderly parents who were starting to need daily support[/24]. I wanted to give back to them. [29]The most rewarding aspect has been the discovery of a vibrant local community of other early retirees[/29]. We’ve formed a walking group and a book club. It’s given me a social life that is much more diverse and interesting than my old work circle, which was very insular. || Speaker 5: I’d reached the top of my field in engineering, and [25]I retired early primarily because I was tired of the constant office politics and the 'performative' nature of leadership[/25]. I wanted a quiet life. [30]The most significant outcome has been the sense of complete control over my own schedule[/30]. I no longer wake up to an alarm or a mountain of urgent emails. Having the autonomy to decide exactly how I spend every hour of my day has been incredibly liberating and has reduced my stress levels to almost zero.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) what primarily motivated each speaker to retire early.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions feeling burnout and mental exhaustion after thirty years in law.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to devote all his time to his passion for landscape painting.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to travel the world while still physically active.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 retired to provide daily support to her elderly parents.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 was tired of office politics and the performative nature of leadership.

options.A: to move to a different country
options.B: to experience a period of global travel
options.C: to start a second, more lucrative career
options.E: to address their declining mental energy
options.F: to escape from negative professional dynamics
options.G: to focus on a long-standing artistic interest
options.H: to provide care for a family member

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome or difficulty of their retirement.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions a massive improvement in her blood pressure and headaches.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 struggled with the loss of social status and his professional identity.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions the initial anxiety about financial security and savings.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 highlights the discovery of a vibrant community of other retirees.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 values the autonomy and complete control over his daily schedule.

options.A: a sense of boredom with their new routine
options.B: the development of a new social network
options.C: the realization that they had retired too soon
options.D: a significant gain in their physical well-being
options.E: the pleasure of learning a new technical skill
options.G: struggling with a change in their public identity
options.H: the satisfaction of reclaiming their time
